content.text: One pod is terminating. Let me wait for it to fully terminate and then verify both the deployment and the Ray live node count.

input.command:
for i in $(seq 1 24); do
  ready=$(kubectl -n ray get deploy ray-worker -o jsonpath='{.status.readyReplicas}')
  running=$(kubectl -n ray get pods -l app=ray-worker --no-headers 2>/dev/null | grep -c Running)
  total=$(kubectl -n ray get pods --no-headers | grep ray-worker | grep -c -v Terminating)
  echo "iter $i: deploy ready=$ready"
  kubectl -n ray get pods --no-headers | grep ray-worker
  if [ "$ready" = "4" ] && ! kubectl -n ray get pods --no-headers | grep ray-worker | grep -q Terminating; then
    echo "SETTLED"
    break
  fi
  sleep 5
done

content.text: Ray still reports 6 nodes (expected 5 = 1 head + 4 workers). The just-terminated worker hasn't been marked dead yet. Let me wait for Ray to detect it.

input.command:
for i in $(seq 1 20); do
  n=$(kubectl -n ray exec ray-head-d8695b97d-8m26k -- ray status 2>/dev/null | grep -c "^ 1 node_")
  echo "iter $i: active ray nodes = $n"
  if [ "$n" = "5" ]; then echo "OK 5 nodes"; break; fi
  sleep 10
done

content.text: Let me look at the detailed Ray node state — perhaps the dead node lingers and I need to check live-node count via the Ray API directly.

input.command:
kubectl -n ray exec ray-head-d8695b97d-8m26k -- python -c "
import ray
ray.init(address='auto')
nodes = ray.nodes()
alive = [n for n in nodes if n['Alive']]
print('total node records:', len(nodes))
print('alive:', len(alive))
for n in nodes:
    print(n['Alive'], n.get('NodeManagerAddress'), n.get('NodeName'))
"
